title: Loan Calculator — Monthly Payment & Total Interest
description: Calculate your monthly loan payment, total interest paid, and full cost of any fixed-rate loan. Works for personal loans, auto loans, and more.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How is the monthly interest rate derived from the annual rate?
faq.a1: The monthly interest rate is the annual rate divided by 12. A 6% annual rate becomes 0.5% per month (0.06 ÷ 12 = 0.005). This monthly rate is used in the amortization formula. Note that this produces an effective annual rate slightly above 6% due to monthly compounding — specifically 6.168% APY — which is why APR and APY differ on installment loans.
faq.q2: What does total interest represent and how can I reduce it?
faq.a2: Total interest is the cumulative sum of all interest payments over the loan term — calculated as (monthly payment × number of months) minus the principal. On a $15,000 auto loan at 7% over 60 months, total interest is $2,799. To reduce it: choose a shorter loan term, make a larger down payment to reduce principal, or make extra payments toward principal. Even one extra payment per year reduces total interest significantly.
faq.q3: What is the difference between a personal loan and an auto loan calculator?
faq.a3: Mathematically, both use the same amortization formula. The differences are in typical interest rates (auto loans average 5–8%, personal loans 8–15% for good credit), loan terms (auto: 36–72 months, personal: 12–84 months), and whether the loan is secured (auto — collateral is the car) or unsecured (personal — no collateral). This calculator works for any fixed-rate installment loan regardless of purpose.
faq.q4: What does the monthly payment include?
faq.a4: The monthly payment covers both principal reduction and interest. In the early months, most of the payment goes to interest — this is called front-loading, a consequence of the amortization formula. Over time, as the balance decreases, more goes to principal. For a $10,000 loan at 6% over 36 months ($304/month): month 1 splits into $250 interest and $54 principal. By month 30, it is $29 interest and $275 principal. The payment amount stays the same, but the split changes every month.
faq.q5: Is there a recommended standard for loan interest calculation?
faq.a5: Yes. The standard amortization formula used here is recognized by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) and is the legally required calculation method for disclosed APR on U.S. consumer loans under the Truth in Lending Act (TILA). All U.S. banks and credit unions use this formula for fixed-rate installment loans.
faq.q6: How does loan term length affect total cost?
faq.a6: Longer loan terms lower monthly payments but dramatically increase total interest paid. Example — $20,000 at 7%: 36 months = $617/month, $2,207 total interest. 60 months = $396/month, $3,761 total interest. 84 months = $301/month, $5,249 total interest. The 84-month loan has a payment 51% lower than the 36-month loan, but costs $3,042 more in total interest. Always choose the shortest term whose payment you can comfortably afford.
faq.q7: What happens if I make extra principal payments?
faq.a7: Extra principal payments reduce the outstanding balance immediately, which means future interest is charged on a smaller base — reducing both the total interest paid and the loan payoff timeline. On a $20,000 loan at 7% over 60 months, making one extra $396 payment per year toward principal reduces total interest from $3,761 to approximately $3,200 and pays the loan off 6 months early. Even $50 extra per month on a mortgage can save thousands and years.
faq.q8: What credit score do I need for a good interest rate?
faq.a8: Interest rates vary significantly by credit score. For personal loans: Excellent credit (750+) typically qualifies for 6–10% APR; Good (700–749) for 10–15%; Fair (650–699) for 15–25%; Poor (below 650) often 25–36% or denial. For auto loans, rates are generally lower due to secured collateral: Excellent credit averages 4–6%, Good 6–9%, Fair 9–14%. A difference of 5% APR on a $15,000 loan over 48 months changes total interest from ~$1,600 to ~$3,900 — nearly $2,300 in extra cost.

content.whatIs:
A loan calculator computes your monthly payment, total interest paid, and total cost for any fixed-rate installment loan — including personal loans, auto loans, student loans, and small business loans. It uses the standard amortization formula recognized by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) and required by U.S. law for APR disclosure on all consumer credit products.

In a fixed-rate installment loan, you borrow a lump sum (the principal), agree to a fixed interest rate and loan term, and repay through equal monthly payments that fully retire the debt by the end of the term. Each payment is split between interest (the lender's fee for providing the loan) and principal reduction (paying down the balance). Because interest is charged on the remaining balance, the split shifts over time — early payments are mostly interest, later payments are mostly principal.

Knowing your true loan cost before borrowing is essential for informed financial decisions. A seemingly affordable monthly payment can hide a very high total cost when stretched over a long term. This calculator makes the full picture visible — monthly payment, total interest, and total cost — so you can compare loan offers accurately and choose the option that minimizes your total borrowing cost.
content.formula:
Standard Amortization Formula (CFPB / TILA standard):
M = P × [r(1+r)^n] ÷ [(1+r)^n − 1]

Where:
  M = monthly payment
  P = principal (loan amount)
  r = monthly interest rate (annual rate ÷ 12)
  n = total number of monthly payments (term in months)

Total Payment  = M × n
Total Interest  = (M × n) − P
content.example:
Example 1 — Personal loan:
$10,000 at 9% APR over 36 months.
r = 0.09/12 = 0.0075, n = 36
M = 10,000 × [0.0075 × (1.0075)^36] / [(1.0075)^36 − 1] = $317.99/month
Total paid: $11,448 | Total interest: $1,448

Example 2 — Auto loan comparison (36 vs 60 months):
$22,000 at 6.5% APR.
36 months: $674/month | Total interest: $2,278
60 months: $430/month | Total interest: $3,796
Longer term saves $244/month but costs $1,518 extra in total interest.

Example 3 — Impact of interest rate (same loan, different credit score):
$15,000 over 48 months.
6% APR: $352/month | Total interest: $1,883
15% APR: $417/month | Total interest: $5,010
Good vs poor credit costs an extra $3,127 on the same loan.
content.howItWorks_1: Enter the loan amount (principal), annual interest rate as a percentage, and loan term in months. For a 5-year auto loan, enter 60 months. For a 3-year personal loan, enter 36. Ensure the rate is the APR (Annual Percentage Rate) as quoted by your lender.
content.howItWorks_2: Click Calculate. The amortization formula instantly computes your fixed monthly payment, the total amount paid over the full term, and the total interest — the cost of borrowing expressed in dollars.
content.howItWorks_3: Compare loan offers by running multiple scenarios: adjust the rate, term, or loan amount and observe how each factor changes total cost. A lower monthly payment from a longer term often hides higher total interest — use total interest as your primary comparison metric, not monthly payment.
content.whyItMatters:
Consumer loans are the second most significant financial commitment most people make after mortgages. Americans collectively carry over $1.6 trillion in auto loan debt and nearly $250 billion in personal loan balances. Understanding the true cost of a loan — not just the monthly payment — is the most important step in responsible borrowing.

Interest rate differences have a compounding effect on total loan cost. The difference between 6% and 12% APR on a $20,000 auto loan over 60 months is $3,200 in extra interest paid — nearly 16% of the loan principal paid purely in additional borrowing costs for the same car. Your credit score is the primary determinant of your interest rate, making credit health one of the most financially impactful personal finance activities: improving your credit score from 'Fair' to 'Good' can save thousands of dollars on any significant loan.

Loan term is equally consequential. Dealers and lenders often advertise monthly payment rather than total cost precisely because it conceals how expensive long-term financing is. A $35,000 car financed at 7% over 72 months has a monthly payment of $533 — but you will pay $38,400 over the life of the loan: $3,400 more than the car's price. This calculator puts total cost front and center so you can make the decision with full information.
content.limitations:
This calculator computes principal and interest only. For auto loans, the actual monthly obligation may include gap insurance, extended warranty costs, or taxes and fees financed into the loan amount. For personal loans, origination fees (typically 1–8% of the loan amount, deducted from the disbursed amount) effectively raise the real APR above the stated rate — confirm whether quoted rates include or exclude origination fees.

This calculator assumes a fixed interest rate for the entire loan term. Variable-rate personal loans or HELOCs (home equity lines of credit) have rates that reset periodically — the amortization formula does not apply to those products in the same way. Similarly, balloon payment loans — which have small monthly payments followed by a large lump-sum payment at maturity — cannot be accurately modeled with standard amortization.

For student loans, this calculator works for fixed-rate private loans but not for federal income-driven repayment plans (IDR, PAYE, SAVE), which base monthly payments on income rather than loan balance and include forgiveness provisions after 10–25 years. Always consult your loan servicer or a certified financial counselor for student loan repayment strategy.
content.tips_1: Improve your credit score before applying for a major loan. Even moving from 680 to 720 can reduce your interest rate by 1–2 percentage points on a personal or auto loan. Pay down credit card balances below 30% of the limit, dispute any errors on your credit report, and avoid new hard inquiries in the 3–6 months before applying.
content.tips_2: Choose the shortest loan term you can comfortably afford. Every extra month extends interest charges on the declining balance. Run this calculator with different terms to see the total interest impact — the difference between a 48-month and 72-month term on a $25,000 loan at 7% is over $2,400 in extra interest for the same amount borrowed.
content.tips_3: Make extra principal payments whenever possible. Even $50 extra per month on a loan accelerates payoff and reduces total interest significantly. Contact your lender to confirm that extra payments are applied directly to principal, not future interest — most lenders allow this but it should be confirmed, especially for auto loans.
content.tips_4: Get at least three competing loan quotes before accepting any offer. Banks, credit unions, online lenders, and peer-to-peer platforms each price risk differently. Credit unions in particular often offer rates 1–2% lower than commercial banks for members. Compare APR (not just interest rate), loan fees, and prepayment penalty terms across all quotes before deciding.
